<summary>Tool use: **run_terminal_command_v2** • Run command: for f in /home/jorap/Documents/GitHub/jorap.com/content/english/notes/*.md; do
  base=$(basename &#34;$f&#34;)

  echo &#34;===== $base =====&#34;

done</summary>
```bash
for f in /home/jorap/Documents/GitHub/jorap.com/content/english/notes/*.md; do
  base=$(basename "$f")
  if [[ "$base" == "_index.md" ]]; then continue; fi
  echo "===== $base ====="
  awk 'BEGIN{p=0} /^---$/{p++; next} p>=2{print}' "$f" | head -5
  echo
done
```

```
===== active-knowledge-curation.md =====

Saving links isn't curation. I revisit monthly, prune what I wouldn't save again today, and connect what I actually care about.

Once a month I open old folders and ask: would I save this again today? If not, I delete or merge. Curation is [[Periodic Knowledge Review]] with teeth - fewer notes, stronger links, notes I actually open.


===== advantages-of-digital-gardens.md =====

I prefer a garden over polishing every thought into a finished essay - messy, linked, and alive beats a portfolio of sealed posts.

Finished essays age fast. Garden notes can sit half-right, get linked, get updated. Readers see the thinking, not just the conclusion. That's closer to how ideas actually grow. See [[Drafting in Public]] and [[Digital Garden]].


===== analog-capture-tools.md =====

I love my wiki, but sometimes paper wins - meetings, sermons, the pocket notebook that survives a dead phone battery.

Paper goes through the same rule as digital: one trusted inbox, processed weekly. I photograph or transcribe into [[Capture]] - the medium changes, the pipe doesn't. Meetings and sermons are where analog still wins for me.


===== anti-fragile-systems.md =====

Fragile systems break when an app shuts down. I build around plain files, open formats, and exports I actually test.

If an app vanished tomorrow, could I still read my notes? Markdown in a folder, exports I test twice a year, no single gatekeeper. Stress should surface gaps, not erase years of work. See [[Future-Proofing Knowledge]] and [[Local-first Software]].


===== associative-linking.md =====

Folders sort. Links think. I link so notes meet without me remembering every filename.

When I write a note, I link to two neighbors minimum - what it extends, what it contradicts. Filenames fade; relationships stick. That's how [[Zettelkasten]] and a [[Digital Garden]] stay walkable without a perfect folder tree.


===== atomic-design-for-notes.md =====

I borrowed Brad Frost's atomic de
… (output truncated)
```

<tool-use data-tool-type="shell" data-tool-name="run_terminal_command_v2"><details>
<summary>Tool use: **run_terminal_command_v2** • Run command: for f in /home/jorap/Documents/GitHub/jorap.com/content/english/notes/*.md; do
  base=$(basename &#34;$f&#34;)
  [[ &#34;$base&#34; == &#34;_index.md&#34; ]] &amp;&amp; continue
  awk &#39;BEGIN{p=0} /^---$/{p++; next} p&gt;=2{print}&#39; &#34;$f&#34; | head -3 | grep -q . || echo &#34;EMPTY: $base&#34;
done
# List files not shown in truncated output
ls /home/jorap/Documents/GitHub/jorap.com/content/english/notes/*.md | wc -l</summary>
```bash
for f in /home/jorap/Documents/GitHub/jorap.com/content/english/notes/*.md; do
  base=$(basename "$f")
  [[ "$base" == "_index.md" ]] && continue
  awk 'BEGIN{p=0} /^---$/{p++; next} p>=2{print}' "$f" | head -3 | grep -q . || echo "EMPTY: $base"
done
# List files not shown in truncated output
ls /home/jorap/Documents/GitHub/jorap.com/content/english/notes/*.md | wc -l
```

_**Agent (default)**_

<think><details><summary>Thought Process</summary>
The goal is to rewrite note definitions so they are easier to explain and remember.

A two-line format will work best: a plain definition on the first line, then a personal memory hook on the second. Landing pages, meta guides, and utility pages will be skipped. Concept notes will be updated.

A Python script will batch-update the note files. Each definition will begin with a bold term, followed by a plain explanation, then a memory hook using contrast or acronyms. An example is being drafted for Active Knowledge Curation.

Specific memory techniques are now chosen: acronyms, contrast pairs, analogies, and the "would I save this again today?" test. The PKM definition will be rewritten as a short formula: CODE (Capture, Organize, Distill, Express). A comprehensive update is planned.

About seventy concept notes will be updated in batches. The Maps of Content note will get a one-liner opening: "A Map of Content (MOC) is a hand-written table of contents — a hub note that lists links, not folders."

The getting-started guide will stay as-is because its section headers are intentional.

Drafting has begun. The first updated definition is for active knowledge curation: "revisiting notes and cutting what you wouldn't save again," with a memory hook about hoarding links and the monthly "save again today?" test.

The digital garden definition contrasts finished posts with growing notes, highlighting that readers see the thinking, not just the conclusion. Paper capture is framed as the same trusted pipe with a different medium, winning when screens fail or feel rude.

Paper capture keeps the same processing rule even when the medium changes. Anti-fragile notes survive app death through plain files, open formats, and tested exports. Folders sort; links think. Connect notes by meaning, not filename.
</details></think>

_**Agent (default)**_

Updating all atomic concept notes with clearer, memorable definitions—plain “X = Y” openers, contrast pairs, and simple memory hooks—while keeping JoRap voice and wikilinks.

The script removed a blank line before `## See also`, causing a formatting issue.

The blank line disappears because the elaboration text lacks a trailing newline, causing the replacement to merge directly into the next heading. This needs fixing across all files.

The fix is to insert two newlines before the elaboration and strip the leading newline from the tail, ensuring a blank line remains before `## See also`.
</details></think>

<summary>Tool use: **run_terminal_command_v2** • Run command: cd /home/jorap/Documents/GitHub/jorap.com &amp;&amp; python3 -c &#34;
from pathlib import Path
import re
notes = Path(&#39;content/english/notes&#39;)
for path in notes.glob(&#39;*.md&#39;):
    text = path.read_text()
    fixed = re.sub(r&#39;([^\n])\n(## See also)&#39;, r&#39;\1\n\n\2&#39;, text)
    if fixed != text:
        path.write_text(fixed)
        print(&#39;fixed blank line:&#39;, path.name)
&#34;</summary>
```bash
cd /home/jorap/Documents/GitHub/jorap.com && python3 -c "
from pathlib import Path
import re
notes = Path('content/english/notes')
for path in notes.glob('*.md'):
    text = path.read_text()
    fixed = re.sub(r'([^\n])\n(## See also)', r'\1\n\n\2', text)
    if fixed != text:
        path.write_text(fixed)
        print('fixed blank line:', path.name)
"
```

_**Agent (default)**_

Updated **73 concept notes** with a consistent, easier-to-remember definition format. Left `_index.md` and `getting-started.md` alone — those are section/meta pages, not atomic definitions.

### New pattern

Each note now opens with a **bold formula** you can quote in one breath:

```16:18:content/english/notes/active-knowledge-curation.md
**Active curation** = revisiting notes on a schedule and cutting what you wouldn't save again today.

Hoarding links isn't curation. Once a month I open old folders and ask: save again today? If not, delete or merge. That's [[Periodic Knowledge Review]] with teeth - fewer notes, stronger links, notes I actually open.
```

The second paragraph adds a **memory hook** (contrast, question, acronym, or analogy) plus how you use it. Frontmatter `description` fields were aligned to match.

### Examples of the hooks used

| Style | Example |
|--------|---------|
| Contrast | **Folders sort. Links think.** |
| Pair | **Fleeting notes catch sparks. Evergreen notes hold truth.** |
| Acronym | **PARA** = Projects, Areas, Resources, Archive |
| Loop | **PKM** = capture, organize, distill, express |
| Analogy | **Garage vs showroom** = messy workshop for notes, polished blog for guests |
| Filter question | **Signal vs noise** = save only what changes action or belief this month |

`maps-of-content.md` keeps its full index; only the intro definition was refreshed. Hugo build passes with no broken wikilinks.

Want the same treatment on `getting-started.md`, or is the current how-to format fine there?

---

_**User**_

yes

---

Updated `getting-started.md` with the same pattern:

```16:22:content/english/notes/getting-started.md
**Notes garden** = one claim per page, linked with `[[wikilinks]]`, browsable at [/notes/](/notes/).

Public when `draft: false`; hidden while `draft: true`. Each note opens with a definition you can quote in one breath - the graph and backlinks do the organizing. Start at [[Maps of Content]] or walk through the steps below.

## Atomic rule

**One note = one claim** you'd cite in conversation. No `##` sections, no "bottom line" - link out instead. See [[Atomic Notes]].
```

The how-to sections (create, link, explore, import, publish) are unchanged. The atomic rule section got the same bold formula treatment, and em dashes in the feature list were swapped for hyphens to match your voice rules.
